codeReview.summary: Solid implementation of all 5 endpoints (GET/PATCH /api/user/profile, POST /api/user/export, DELETE /api/user/account, GET /api/audit) with 43 comprehensive tests. Code follows existing codebase patterns consistently: Zod validation, standard error envelope, getRequestContext auth checks, fire-and-forget audit events, cursor-based pagination via shared utilities. All feature requirements and expectedBehavior items are met. The DELETE account handler correctly awaits the audit event insertion BEFORE cascade deletion. Active share counting correctly excludes revoked and expired shares. Export properly decrypts health data. Audit endpoint supports all required filters with human-readable descriptions.

issues.description: HTML stripping uses a simple regex (/<[^>]*>/g) which handles common cases but could miss edge cases with malformed HTML (e.g., unclosed tags, attribute-based vectors). For an MVP display_name field that is stored server-side and not rendered as raw HTML, this is adequate. A dedicated library (e.g., sanitize-html) would be more robust for production hardening.

The export endpoint loads ALL user health data into memory for sequential decryption in a for-loop. This is explicitly acceptable per the spec ('For MVP, return the data inline (sync) since <500 users'), but should be noted for future scaling — a streaming/batched approach would be needed at scale.

The audit event in PATCH profile is fire-and-forget (no await, .catch logs error). If the audit insert fails, the profile update still succeeds silently. This is consistent with ALL other endpoints in the codebase (shares, connections, viewer, health-data) so it's a project-wide pattern, not a bug. The DELETE account handler correctly awaits the audit event since it must persist before cascade.

sharedStateObservations.observation: Worker acknowledged deviating from the red-green-refactor TDD approach in the backend-worker skill (step 3: 'Write tests FIRST'). Worker implemented routes and tests close together instead of writing failing tests first. The deviation was pragmatic given clear patterns from existing code, but the skill's procedure is clear about test-first. This is a minor procedural deviation that didn't affect quality.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: handoff.skillFeedback.suggestedChanges: 'The backend-worker skill suggests writing tests FIRST (red phase). I implemented the routes and tests close together since the patterns were clear from existing code, but did verify all tests passed after implementation.'

sharedStateObservations.observation: .factory/library/ directory is empty — no architecture notes have been created by any worker yet. The backend-worker skill (step 7) instructs workers to 'Update shared knowledge' if they discover patterns or gotchas. While this feature didn't uncover novel patterns, earlier features in the mission likely should have started populating this directory. Not specific to this worker.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Glob for .factory/library/**/* returned no files. Skill step 7: 'If you discover patterns, gotchas, or environment details that future workers should know, add them to .factory/library/architecture.md or .factory/library/environment.md.'

sharedStateObservations.observation: The fire-and-forget audit event pattern (db.insert().values().catch()) is used consistently across all endpoints but is not documented in AGENTS.md. Future workers implementing new endpoints should know this is the established pattern (with the exception of DELETE account which must await before cascade). This could be added to AGENTS.md under Coding Conventions.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Pattern found in: shares/route.ts:151, shares/[id]/route.ts:208,279, connections/[id]/sync/route.ts:143, viewer/validate/route.ts:124, viewer/data/route.ts:316, health-data/route.ts:299, user/export/route.ts:152, user/profile/route.ts:169. DELETE account (user/account/route.ts:79) correctly uses await.

summary: All 5 endpoints are correctly implemented with comprehensive test coverage (43 tests). The implementation follows existing codebase conventions consistently — auth via getRequestContext, Zod validation, standard error envelope, fire-and-forget audit events, and cursor pagination via shared utilities. Three non-blocking issues noted: (1) simple regex HTML stripping is adequate for MVP but could be hardened, (2) in-memory export is fine for <500 users but won't scale, (3) fire-and-forget audit pattern is consistent with codebase but undocumented. No blocking issues found. PASS.
